file_name: Ennaji et al. - 2023 - Malignant melanoma detection using multi-scale image decomposition and a new ensemble-learning schem.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning for malignant melanoma detection in dermoscopic images. Specifically, it employs an ensemble-learning scheme with three separate Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers based on shape, color, and texture features, combined via a majority voting approach. The model was validated on the PH2 database.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new ensemble-learning scheme for malignant melanoma detection. It details a specific methodology involving multi-scale image decomposition using Partial Differential Equations (PDE) and three separate SVM classifiers. The study includes a simulation process, validation on the PH2 database, and reports original quantitative results (98% accuracy, 95% recall, 95% precision), distinguishing it from a review paper which would only summarize prior literature.

title: Malignant melanoma detection using multi‑scale image decomposition and a new ensemble‑learning scheme
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system for detecting malignant melanoma in dermoscopic images using multi-scale image decomposition and an ensemble-learning scheme. The methodology employs Partial Differential Equations (PDE) to decompose images into structure and texture components, facilitating the extraction of shape, color, and texture features. Three separate Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers are designed for each feature type, with the final diagnosis determined by a majority voting approach. The model was validated and tested on the public PH2 database. The proposed approach achieved a classification accuracy of 98%, a recall of 95%, and a precision of 95%. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the ensemble method compared to using individual feature sets or other state-of-the-art methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection and diagnosis in the proposed ensemble-learning scheme. The study focuses on distinguishing malignant melanoma from non-melanoma lesions using the PH2 database.

The negative class used in the binary classification task. The paper refers to these as 'non-melanoma' in confusion matrices and tables, and occasionally as 'benign melanoma' in the methodology description (likely meaning benign lesions or non-malignant cases). The PH2 database typically contains melanoma and two types of benign nevi (common nevus and atypical nevus), which are grouped here as non-melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic label (Malignant melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to detect malignant melanoma in dermoscopic images. The model uses an ensemble-learning scheme with three Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers based on shape, color, and texture features, combined via a majority voting approach. The task is explicitly defined as classifying images into 'Melanoma' or 'Non-Melanoma' categories, validated on the PH2 database with reported accuracy of 98%, recall of 95%, and precision of 95%.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Database
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image database obtained through collaboration between the Department of Dermatology in the Pedro Hispano Hospital of Matosinhos, Portugal, and Porto University, Técnico Lisboa. It contains dermoscopic images of skin type II or III with a resolution of 768x560 pixels.

use.training: 80% of the dataset (160 images) was used for the training set.
use.testing: 20% of the dataset (40 images) was used for the testing set.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, recall, precision, specificity) and confusion matrices on the PH2 database.

PH2 Database.notes: The proposed ensemble approach outperformed individual feature classifiers and the fusion of all features into a single classifier. The study compared these results against state-of-the-art methods in Table 11, noting superior performance on the PH2 dataset.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble-learning scheme comprising three separate Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers combined via a majority voting approach.

architecture_details.preprocessing: Multi-scale image decomposition based on Partial Differential Equations (PDE) using the Osher-Vese model to separate structure (object) and texture components.
architecture_details.segmentation: Otsu’s thresholding applied to the object component (u) to detect regions of interest (lesion and safe skin).
feature_extraction: Shape features extracted from the segmented lesion.
Texture features extracted from the texture component (v) obtained by PDE decomposition.
Color features extracted from the original images.
classifiers: SVM classifier based on shape features.
SVM classifier based on color features.
SVM classifier based on texture features.
architecture_details.fusion_method: Majority voting approach to combine predictions from the three SVM classifiers.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Higher-Order Standardized Moment Induced-Local Binary Patterns
preprocessing_techniques: Multi-scale image decomposition based on Partial Differential Equations (PDE) using the Osher-Vese model to separate structure (object) and texture components
Otsu’s thresholding applied to the object component for segmentation of regions of interest (lesion and safe skin)
conclusions: The proposed ensemble-learning scheme, which combines three separate Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers based on shape, color, and texture features via a majority voting approach, achieved a classification accuracy of 98%, recall of 95%, and precision of 95% on the PH2 database.

The use of multi-scale image decomposition based on Partial Differential Equations (PDE) effectively isolated the object and texture components, facilitating accurate segmentation of the lesion and safe skin regions for feature extraction.

The proposed ensemble approach outperformed individual feature-based classifiers (color, shape, texture) and a single classifier using fused features, demonstrating that combining multiple feature sets improves diagnostic performance.
The proposed framework demonstrated superiority over several state-of-the-art methods reported in the literature, achieving higher accuracy (98%) and specificity (98.75%) on the PH2 dataset.

The study concludes that the proposed approach is promising for assisting dermatologists in the early and accurate detection of malignant melanoma, addressing limitations of previous methods that relied on simplistic features or lacked robust validation.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single public dataset (PH2), which lacks external validation on diverse, multi-center, or larger datasets (e.g., ISIC), limiting generalizability.
The dataset is small (200 images total) and imbalanced, with 40 melanoma cases and 160 non-melanoma cases (80 common moles, 80 atypical moles), potentially biasing performance metrics.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) to validate the superiority of the proposed method over state-of-the-art comparisons.
The methodology relies on handcrafted features (shape, color, texture) extracted via PDE decomposition and SVMs, which may be less robust than deep learning approaches for complex lesion variations.
The 'non-melanoma' class groups two distinct benign lesion types (common and atypical nevi), which may mask performance differences between benign subtypes.
No data leakage risks are explicitly addressed, though the fixed 80/20 split on a small dataset requires careful interpretation of overfitting potential.
Clinical relevance is claimed but not supported by prospective clinical trials or comparison with dermatologist performance on the same dataset.
